content.text:
<user_query>
@c:\Users\bhara\.cursor\projects\e-Trilogy-Backtrace-target-TI\terminals\4.txt:7-24 why am I getting this error and how to resolve it
</user_query>

`Get-ECRLoginCommand` is from AWS Tools for PowerShell, not the AWS CLI — checking what's installed on your machine.

[REDACTED]

input.command: Get-Command Get-ECRLoginCommand -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue; Get-Command aws -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | Select-Object Name, Source; aws --version 2>&1
input.description: Check AWS PowerShell module and AWS CLI

content.text:
## Why you see this error

`Get-ECRLoginCommand` is **not** part of Docker or the AWS CLI. It comes from **AWS Tools for PowerShell** (modules like `AWSPowerShell` or `AWS.Tools.ECR`). PowerShell is telling you that cmdlet does not exist because that module is not installed (or not loaded) in your session.

On this machine, `aws` is also not available in PATH, so you likely do not have a working AWS CLI setup either.

## How to fix it

Pick **one** of these approaches.

### Option A — AWS CLI (recommended)

1. Install [AWS CLI v2 for Windows](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/userguide/getting-started-install.html).
2. Open a **new** PowerShell window (so PATH updates).
3. Configure credentials if needed: `aws configure`
4. Log in to ECR:

```powershell
aws ecr get-login-password --region eu-west-1 | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin 346945241475.dkr.ecr.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com
```

Use the same region as your ECR registry (`eu-west-1` here).

### Option B — AWS Tools for PowerShell (what your command expects)

```powershell
Install-Module -Name AWS.Tools.ECR -Scope CurrentUser -Force
Import-Module AWS.Tools.ECR
(Get-ECRLoginCommand -Region eu-west-1).Password | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin 346945241475.dkr.ecr.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com
```

You also need AWS credentials configured (e.g. `aws configure` or environment variables) so the module can call ECR.

## Prerequisites (both options)

- **Docker Desktop** running.
- **AWS credentials** with permission to `ecr:GetAuthorizationToken` (and push/pull on that repo if you are building/pushing).
- Correct **account and region** (`346945241475`, `eu-west-1`).

## Quick check

After installing AWS CLI:

```powershell
aws --version
aws sts get-caller-identity
```

If those work, the `aws ecr get-login-password ...` login command should work.

**Summary:** The error is because you ran a PowerShell-only ECR cmdlet without installing AWS Tools for PowerShell. Install AWS CLI and use `aws ecr get-login-password`, or install the `AWS.Tools.ECR` module and keep using `Get-ECRLoginCommand`.

[REDACTED]

<user_query>

E:\Trilogy\Backtrace_target_TI>aws --version
aws-cli/2.34.57 Python/3.14.5 Windows/11 exe/AMD64

E:\Trilogy\Backtrace_target_TI>aws sts get-caller-identity

aws: [ERROR]: An error occurred (NoCredentials): Unable to locate credentials. You can configure credentials by running "aws login".

E:\Trilogy\Backtrace_target_TI>aws ecr get-login-password --region eu-west-1 | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin 346945241475.dkr.ecr.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com

aws: [ERROR]: An error occurred (NoCredentials): Unable to locate credentials. You can configure credentials by running "aws login".
Error: Cannot perform an interactive login from a non TTY device

E:\Trilogy\Backtrace_target_TI>(Get-ECRLoginCommand).Password | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin 346945241475.dkr.ecr.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com
.Password was unexpected at this time.

what should I need to do now
</user_query>

AWS CLI is installed; credentials aren't configured. Checking the project for any AWS setup notes.

[REDACTED]

You’re past the “CLI not installed” step. Two separate issues remain: **no AWS credentials**, and **running PowerShell syntax in CMD**.

## 1. Configure AWS credentials first

`NoCredentials` means the CLI has nothing to use. Fix that before ECR or Docker login.

### If your org uses SSO / “Sign in with AWS” (common with Trilogy-style setups)

In **PowerShell** or **CMD**:

```text
aws login
```

Follow the browser sign-in. Then verify:

```text
aws sts get-caller-identity
```

You should see `Account`, `UserId`, and `Arn` — not `NoCredentials`.

### If you have access keys (IAM user)

```text
aws configure
```

Enter:

- AWS Access Key ID  
- AWS Secret Access Key  
- Default region: `eu-west-1`  
- Default output: `json` (or leave blank)

Then:

```text
aws sts get-caller-identity
```

### If someone gave you a named profile

```text
set AWS_PROFILE=your-profile-name
aws sts get-caller-identity
```

(In PowerShell: `$env:AWS_PROFILE = "your-profile-name"`.)

**Do not continue to ECR until `get-caller-identity` succeeds.**

If you don’t have SSO access or keys, ask whoever owns account `346945241475` for local access (SSO permission set or IAM user with ECR push/pull).

---

## 2. Log in to ECR (after credentials work)

Use **CMD** or **PowerShell** — both work with the AWS CLI command:

```text
aws ecr get-login-password --region eu-west-1 | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin 346945241475.dkr.ecr.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com
```

Expected: `Login Succeeded`.

The earlier `Cannot perform an interactive login from a non TTY device` happened because `aws` failed (no password), so Docker got an empty/failed pipe. It should go away once credentials are set.

---

## 3. Why `(Get-ECRLoginCommand)` failed

`.Password was unexpected at this time` means you ran that in **CMD**, not PowerShell. Parentheses and `.Password` are PowerShell-only.

- **Stick with AWS CLI** (you already have it): use `aws ecr get-login-password` — no PowerShell module needed.  
- **Or** open **PowerShell** and install the module if you really want the old cmdlet (usually unnecessary).

---

## 4. Typical push flow after login

From `E:\Trilogy\Backtrace_target_TI`:

```text
docker build -t backtrace-target-ti:local .
docker tag backtrace-target-ti:local 346945241475.dkr.ecr.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/<repo-name>:<tag>
docker push 346945241475.dkr.ecr.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/<repo-name>:<tag>
```

Replace `<repo-name>` and `<tag>` with your real ECR repository (often in GitHub secrets as `ECR_REPOSITORY` for this project’s deploy workflow).

---

## Checklist

| Step | Command | Success looks like |
|------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | `aws login` or `aws configure` | Completes without error |
| 2 | `aws sts get-caller-identity` | JSON with account `346945241475` (or your allowed account) |
| 3 | `aws ecr get-login-password ... \| docker login ...` | `Login Succeeded` |
| 4 | `docker push ...` | Upload progress |

**Next action for you:** run `aws login` (or `aws configure` if you have keys), then `aws sts get-caller-identity`. Paste that output (redact account ID if you prefer) if it still fails — the error message will tell us whether it’s SSO, wrong profile, or missing ECR permissions.

[REDACTED]

You are able to change the region in the CLI at any time with the command "aws configure set region NEW_REGION".
AWS Region [us-east-1]:

what should I do now
</user_query>

For your setup, enter **`eu-west-1`** and press Enter.

Your ECR registry is already in that region (`346945241475.dkr.ecr.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com`), so the CLI default should match where your images live. **`us-east-1`** is only the generic default; using it here can cause confusing errors later (wrong region for ECR/API calls).

After login finishes:

1. **Verify credentials**
   ```text
   aws sts get-caller-identity
   ```

2. **Set region explicitly** (so you are not asked every time)
   ```text
   aws configure set region eu-west-1
   ```

3. **Log in to ECR**
   ```text
   aws ecr get-login-password --region eu-west-1 | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin 346945241475.dkr.ecr.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com
   ```

If your team told you a different region for this account, use that instead — but for this repo and registry, **`eu-west-1`** is the right choice.

[REDACTED]

}

E:\Trilogy\Backtrace_target_TI>(Get-ECRLoginCommand).Password | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin 346945241475.dkr.ecr.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com
.Password was unexpected at this time.

what should I do now
</user_query>

Credentials are working. The remaining error is from using PowerShell syntax in CMD — checking the repo for the ECR repository name.

[REDACTED]

You're set on AWS auth. The `(Get-ECRLoginCommand)` line is the problem — **ignore it**. That cmdlet only works in PowerShell with the AWS PowerShell module. In **CMD**, `(Get-ECRLoginCommand).Password` is invalid syntax, which causes `.Password was unexpected at this time`.

## What to run now

Stay in the same **CMD** window and use the AWS CLI:

```text
aws ecr get-login-password --region eu-west-1 | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin 346945241475.dkr.ecr.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com
```

You should see: **`Login Succeeded`**

## Then build and push (example)

Replace `<repo-name>` with your real ECR repository name (from your team, GitHub secret `ECR_REPOSITORY`, or the AWS Console → ECR → Repositories):

```text
cd /d E:\Trilogy\Backtrace_target_TI

docker build -t backtrace-target-ti:local .

docker tag backtrace-target-ti:local 346945241475.dkr.ecr.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/<repo-name>:latest

docker push 346945241475.dkr.ecr.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/<repo-name>:latest
```

## Optional: list ECR repos in your account

```text
aws ecr describe-repositories --region eu-west-1 --query "repositories[].repositoryName" --output table
```

Use one of those names for `<repo-name>`.

## If login or push fails

| Error | Likely cause |
|--------|----------------|
| `AccessDenied` on `get-login-password` | Role needs ECR permissions |
| `repository does not exist` | Wrong repo name or region |
| `denied: User is not authorized` on push | Need `ecr:PutImage` / push rights on that repo |

Your role `RAM-AWS-Prod-DevFactory-devflows-Admin` should usually be enough; if push fails, ask your platform team for the exact ECR repo name and push permissions.

**Summary:** Credentials are good — run the `aws ecr get-login-password ... | docker login ...` line in CMD, not `Get-ECRLoginCommand`.

[REDACTED]

content.text: [REDACTED]

Identifying issues:
1. Browser used GET — routes are POST-only, so Express returned 404 without hitting failure handlers (no `req.log.error`).
2. `request_end` always logs at INFO; only the message changes for 5xx.

POST to the deployed host returns 500 and should emit ERROR logs. Fixing logging so 4xx/5xx use appropriate levels and adding a 404 handler.

input.description: Trigger failure endpoints on deployed host

content.text:
## What happened in your CloudWatch logs

Two separate issues:

### 1. Wrong HTTP method (why you got 404, not a failure)

Your logs show `"method": "GET"` on `/fail/code-bug`. All failure routes are **POST only**:

```107:110:e:\Trilogy\Backtrace_target_TI\src\routes\failures.js
router.post("/fail/code-bug", codeBugHandler);
router.post("/fail/dependency", dependencyFailureHandler);
router.post("/fail/saturation", saturationHandler);
router.post("/fail/random", randomFailureHandler);
```

Opening `http://54.194.184.219:3000/fail/code-bug` in Chrome sends **GET**, so Express never runs the failure handler. You get a 404 and only `request_start` / `request_end` — no `PAYMENT_CODE_BUG` error.

### 2. Old logging always used INFO on `request_end`

`request_end` was always logged with `req.log.info()`, even for 404/500. The message changed for 5xx, but **`level` stayed INFO**. That’s why everything looked like INFO in CloudWatch.

---

## I called your deployed host

These work on the **current** deployment (still old logging):

| Request | Result |
|--------|--------|
| `POST /fail/code-bug` | `500` + `{"error":"internal error",...}` |
| `POST /fail/dependency` | `500` |
| `POST /fail/saturation` | `503` + `service overloaded` |
| `GET /fail/code-bug` | `404` (HTML or JSON depending on version) |

So the service is fine — use **POST**, not GET from the browser.

**CMD / PowerShell:**

```text
curl.exe -X POST "http://54.194.184.219:3000/fail/code-bug" -H "content-type: application/json" -d "{}"
curl.exe -X POST "http://54.194.184.219:3000/fail/dependency" -H "content-type: application/json" -d "{}"
curl.exe -X POST "http://54.194.184.219:3000/fail/saturation" -H "content-type: application/json" -d "{}"
```

On the **current** AWS build you should already see an **ERROR** line (`"message":"Request failed"`) for POST failures — but `request_end` may still be INFO until you redeploy the fix below.

---

## Code changes (redeploy to AWS)

1. **`request_end` log level by status**
   - `>= 500` → **ERROR**
   - `>= 400` → **WARN**
   - else → **INFO**

2. **404 handler** — logs **ERROR** `"Route not found"` with `error_code: NOT_FOUND` (instead of silent Express 404).

After redeploy, a browser GET will produce something like:

- `ERROR` — Route not found  
- `WARN` — request_end with `status_code: 404`

A POST to `/fail/code-bug` will produce:

- `ERROR` — Request failed (`PAYMENT_CODE_BUG`, stack trace)  
- `ERROR` — request_end with `status_code: 500`

---

## Other fields still `unknown`

| Field | Cause |
|-------|--------|
| `commit_sha` / `build_id` | Docker build needs `COMMIT_SHA` build-arg (see Dockerfile) |
| `asg: backtrace-target-local` | Set `ASG_NAME` in the task definition |
| `service: edge-zuul` on all requests | Default `EDGE_SERVICE_NAME` in middleware (by design for edge-style logs) |

---

## Next steps for you

1. **Redeploy** with the updated `src/middleware/requestContext.js` and `src/server.js`.
2. **Trigger failures with POST** (commands above).
3. In CloudWatch, filter: `level = "ERROR"` or `event = "request_end" AND status_code >= 400`.

If you want browser-friendly testing without curl, we can add `GET` handlers that return a small HTML form or redirect — say if you want that.

[REDACTED]
<user_query>Briefly inform the user about the task result and perform any follow-up actions (if needed). If there's no follow-ups needed, don't explicitly say that.</user_query>

The background start that failed (`node -e "import(...)"` with `&&` in PowerShell) wasn’t needed — the server was started with `npm start` instead, and local tests confirmed the logging changes (ERROR on failures, WARN on 404 `request_end`).

POST calls to `http://54.194.184.219:3000` for `/fail/code-bug`, `/fail/dependency`, and `/fail/saturation` all returned the expected error responses. **Redeploy** so CloudWatch picks up the updated `requestContext.js` and `server.js`; until then, use **POST** (not browser GET) to trigger real failure logs.

[REDACTED]

content.text: [REDACTED]

The app is running locally on **http://localhost:3000** (`npm start` is already up). I hit three scenarios; new lines are at the end of **`e:\Trilogy\Backtrace_target_TI\logs\app.jsonl`** (lines 102–123).

## Commands used

```text
curl.exe http://localhost:3000/healthz
curl.exe -X POST http://localhost:3000/checkout -H "content-type: application/json" -d "{\"userId\":\"user_demo\",\"amount\":29.99,\"currency\":\"USD\"}"
curl.exe http://localhost:3000/fail/code-bug
curl.exe -X POST http://localhost:3000/fail/code-bug -H "content-type: application/json" -d "{}"
```

## What to check manually

Open `logs\app.jsonl` in your editor, or in PowerShell:

```powershell
Get-Content e:\Trilogy\Backtrace_target_TI\logs\app.jsonl -Tail 25
```

Filter by level:

```powershell
Select-String -Path e:\Trilogy\Backtrace_target_TI\logs\app.jsonl -Pattern '"level":"INFO"'  | Select-Object -Last 3
Select-String -Path e:\Trilogy\Backtrace_target_TI\logs\app.jsonl -Pattern '"level":"ERROR"' | Select-Object -Last 3
```

---

### INFO — healthy request (`GET /healthz`, status 200)

Line **107** in `app.jsonl`:

```json
{"level":"INFO","event":"request_end","route":"GET /healthz","status_code":200,"message":"Request completed successfully"}
```

### INFO — happy checkout (`POST /checkout`, status 200)

Line **123** — full multi-service trace with `service_count: 8`, downstream calls, audit event:

```json
{"level":"INFO","event":"request_end","route":"POST /checkout","status_code":200,"duration_ms":345,"service_count":8,"message":"Request completed successfully"}
```

### ERROR — real failure (`POST /fail/code-bug`, status 500)

Lines **113–114**:

```json
{"level":"ERROR","error_code":"PAYMENT_CODE_BUG","status_code":500,"message":"Request failed"}
{"level":"ERROR","event":"request_end","status_code":500,"message":"Request completed with failure"}
```

### BONUS — client error (`GET /fail/code-bug`, status 404)

Lines **109–110** (wrong method, like browser):

```json
{"level":"ERROR","error_code":"NOT_FOUND","message":"Route not found"}
{"level":"WARN","event":"request_end","status_code":404,"message":"Request completed with client error"}
```

---

| Scenario | HTTP | Log levels |
|----------|------|------------|
| `GET /healthz` | 200 | INFO |
| `POST /checkout` | 200 | INFO (many span lines) |
| `POST /fail/code-bug` | 500 | ERROR |
| `GET /fail/code-bug` | 404 | ERROR + WARN |

Logs also print to the terminal where `npm start` is running. To reproduce yourself, run the curl commands above while the server is up.

[REDACTED]

Calling all endpoints on the deployed host in sequence to produce INFO, WARN, and ERROR logs in CloudWatch.

input.command:
$base = "http://18.201.87.239:3000"
$results = @()

function Invoke-Endpoint {
  param($Name, $Method, $Path, $Body = $null)
  $uri = "$base$Path"
  try {
    if ($Body) {
      $r = curl.exe -s -w "`n__HTTP__%{http_code}" -X $Method $uri -H "content-type: application/json" -d $Body
    } else {
      $r = curl.exe -s -w "`n__HTTP__%{http_code}" -X $Method $uri
    }
    $parts = $r -split "`n__HTTP__"
    $code = $parts[-1]
    $body = ($parts[0..($parts.Length-2)] -join "`n").Trim()
    if ($body.Length -gt 120) { $body = $body.Substring(0, 120) + "..." }
    $script:results += [pscustomobject]@{ Step = $script:results.Count + 1; Name = $Name; Method = $Method; Path = $Path; Status = $code; Body = $body }
    Write-Host "[$($script:results.Count)] $Method $Path -> $code"
  } catch {
    $script:results += [pscustomobject]@{ Step = $script:results.Count + 1; Name = $Name; Method = $Method; Path = $Path; Status = "ERR"; Body = $_.Exception.Message }
    Write-Host "[$($script:results.Count)] $Method $Path -> FAILED"
  }
}

Write-Host "=== CloudWatch log generation run ===" -ForegroundColor Cyan
Write-Host "Target: $base`n"

# 1-2: INFO happy path
Invoke-Endpoint "Health check (INFO)" "GET" "/healthz"
Start-Sleep -Milliseconds 300
Invoke-Endpoint "Checkout happy path (INFO spans)" "POST" "/checkout" '{"userId":"cloudwatch_demo","amount":49.99,"currency":"USD"}'
Start-Sleep -Milliseconds 500

# 3: 404 client error (ERROR + WARN if new code deployed)
Invoke-Endpoint "Wrong method GET fail (404)" "GET" "/fail/code-bug"
Start-Sleep -Milliseconds 300

# 4-7: Failure POST endpoints
Invoke-Endpoint "Code bug (500 ERROR)" "POST" "/fail/code-bug" "{}"
Start-Sleep -Milliseconds 300
Invoke-Endpoint "Dependency timeout (504 ERROR)" "POST" "/fail/dependency" "{}"
Start-Sleep -Milliseconds 300
Write-Host "[*] Saturation (~3s CPU)..." -ForegroundColor Yellow
Invoke-Endpoint "Saturation (503 WARN+ERROR)" "POST" "/fail/saturation" "{}"
Start-Sleep -Milliseconds 500

# 8-10: random x3 to hit varied failures
Invoke-Endpoint "Random failure #1" "POST" "/fail/random" "{}"
Start-Sleep -Milliseconds 300
Invoke-Endpoint "Random failure #2" "POST" "/fail/random" "{}"
Start-Sleep -Milliseconds 300
Invoke-Endpoint "Random failure #3" "POST" "/fail/random" "{}"
Start-Sleep -Milliseconds 300

# 11: unknown route
Invoke-Endpoint "Unknown route (404)" "GET" "/does-not-exist"
Start-Sleep -Milliseconds 300

# 12: second health to bookend
Invoke-Endpoint "Health check closing (INFO)" "GET" "/healthz"

Write-Host "`n=== Summary ===" -ForegroundColor Cyan
$results | Format-Table Step, Name, Method, Path, Status -AutoSize
input.description: Call all endpoints on deployed IP in sequence

content.text:
Completed a full pass against **http://18.201.87.239:3000**. In CloudWatch, filter the last **~5 minutes** (or search by `request_id` / route names below).

## Call order (request_start → … → request_end)

| # | Endpoint | HTTP | Expected CloudWatch logs |
|---|----------|------|---------------------------|
| 1 | `GET /healthz` | **200** | INFO: `request_start`, `health_check`, `request_end` (success) |
| 2 | `POST /checkout` | **500** | INFO: `request_start` + some spans; then **ERROR** `Request failed` (checkout is failing on this deploy) |
| 3 | `GET /fail/code-bug` | **404** | INFO `request_start` → ERROR `NOT_FOUND` (if new build) or plain 404 → WARN/INFO `request_end` |
| 4 | `POST /fail/code-bug` | **500** | INFO `request_start`, `business_state_transition` → **ERROR** `PAYMENT_CODE_BUG` → ERROR `request_end` |
| 5 | `POST /fail/dependency` | **504** | INFO spans → **ERROR** `PAYMENT_GATEWAY_TIMEOUT` / ECONNREFUSED → ERROR `request_end` |
| 6 | `POST /fail/saturation` | **503** | INFO `request_start` → WARN `request_slow` → **ERROR** `SERVICE_SATURATION` → WARN/ERROR `request_end` |
| 7 | `POST /fail/random` | **500** | **ERROR** `PAYMENT_CODE_BUG` (code-bug path) |
| 8 | `POST /fail/random` | **500** | **ERROR** `PAYMENT_CODE_BUG` again |
| 9 | `POST /fail/random` | **504** | **ERROR** `PAYMENT_GATEWAY_TIMEOUT` (dependency path) |
| 10 | `GET /does-not-exist` | **404** | ERROR `NOT_FOUND` + client-error `request_end` |
| 11 | `GET /healthz` | **200** | INFO bookend (same as #1) |

---

## Errors generated (for filtering)

| `error_code` | Triggered by | HTTP |
|--------------|----------------|------|
| `PAYMENT_CODE_BUG` | `POST /fail/code-bug`, `POST /fail/random` (×2) | 500 |
| `PAYMENT_GATEWAY_TIMEOUT` | `POST /fail/dependency`, `POST /fail/random` (×1) | 504 |
| `SERVICE_SATURATION` | `POST /fail/saturation` | 503 |
| `NOT_FOUND` | `GET /fail/code-bug`, `GET /does-not-exist` | 404 |
| Checkout failure | `POST /checkout` | 500 (unexpected on this host) |

Also look for:
- **WARN** — `request_slow` (saturation), `request_end` with `status_code` 404/503
- **INFO** — health checks, checkout spans (where checkout didn’t crash), `request_start` on every call

---

## CloudWatch filter tips

```
fields @timestamp, level, event, route, status_code, error_code, message
| filter route like /healthz/ or route like /checkout/ or route like /fail/ or route like /does-not-exist/
| sort @timestamp asc
```

Or by level:
```
| filter level = "ERROR"
```

---

## Note on `POST /checkout`

Checkout returned **500** on this IP (HTML “Internal Server Error”), so you may not get the full happy-path INFO trace (catalog, CDN, audit, etc.). Health and all `/fail/*` endpoints behaved as designed.

If you redeployed the logging fix, 404s should show **ERROR** + **WARN** instead of all **INFO**. If CloudWatch still shows all INFO on 404/500, that instance is still on the older image — redeploy to pick up the changes.

**Member ID on checkout:** `cloudwatch_demo` (header/body) — useful to find that request in logs.
